title: Superspace Concentration and Adversarial Robustness in Quantum Algorithms
authors: Eric Yocam, Christian Yocam, Varghese Vaidyan, Yong Wang, Mahesh Kalappattil, Anthony Rizi
abstract: We study superspace concentration as a quantum resource, formalized through the focus measure F(\r{ho}) = λ_max(\r{ho}_super) - the largest eigenvalue of the reduced superspace state - which quantifies the capacity of a quantum system to concentrate informational weight into a preferred subspace of an extended degree-of-freedom space. We develop a complete resource-theoretic framework around this measure and validate its properties through GPU-accelerated numerical simulation. Analytic decoherence predictions are confirmed to machine precision (1.11 x 10^{-16}) for superspace dimensions dS in {2,4,8,16,32}. Focus monotonicity holds across 10,000 random states with zero violations under four focus-non-generating channels across six system configurations. Focused quantum states resist coherent unitary attacks with significantly greater resilience than standard fidelity predicts, with focus remaining above 0.9 at attack strength ε = 0.302 versus ε = 0.174 for fidelity. We further demonstrate that the focus measure and the U(dS)-asymmetry measure are operationally distinct: asymmetry remains near zero and provides no robustness signal under coherent and targeted attacks while focus tracks spectral concentration and remains robust until ε > 0.3. The connection between Grover's algorithm and superspace concentration is made explicit via the identity F(|ψ_k><ψ_k|) = P(marked), providing a resource-theoretic interpretation of oracle query complexity. Finally, we provide the first numerical characterization of the focus capacity gap ΔF, identifying a log_2(dS) scaling law confirmed for both product and correlated noise channels.
summary: This paper introduces 'superspace concentration' as a novel resource measure quantifying how well quantum information can be concentrated in a preferred subspace. It rigorously proves its properties and demonstrates that this focus measure offers superior resilience against adversarial attacks compared to standard fidelity metrics. The work provides a powerful, theoretically grounded tool for assessing the security and capacity of quantum protocols.
main_problem: The paper develops a resource-theoretic framework around 'superspace concentration' ($F(ho)$) to quantify quantum resources, particularly for understanding adversarial robustness and algorithmic complexity.
main_result: Focus ($F(ho)$) was shown to be operationally distinct from other measures like U(dS)-asymmetry, providing superior resilience against coherent unitary attacks, and its capacity gap scales as $\log_2(d_S)$.
method: The authors use resource theory axioms (monotonicity, etc.) combined with GPU-accelerated numerical simulations to test the behavior of $F(ho)$ under various noise channels and adversarial perturbations.
model_or_system: The system involves a composite Hilbert space $H = H_{	ext{phys}} \otimes H_{	ext{super}}$, where the focus measure is derived from the largest eigenvalue ($\lambda_{\max}$) of the reduced density matrix $ho_{	ext{super}}$ over an extended superspace.
key_observables: Focus Measure ($F(ho) = \lambda_{\max}(ho_{	ext{super}})$), Focus Capacity Gap ($\Delta F$), and comparison against standard fidelity.
important_parameters: Superspace dimension $d_S$, attack strength $\epsilon$, noise parameters $p$ (for depolarizing channels).
main_assumptions: The framework relies on identifying 'Focus-Non-Generating' (FNG) operations, and capacity gap analysis is currently limited to product and correlated noise channels.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the superior robustness of Focus over fidelity under adversarial attacks, confirm the $\log_2(d_S)$ scaling law for the capacity gap, and map out monotonicity violations across various system configurations.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the mathematical definition of $F(ho)$, proves its resource-theoretic properties (monotonicity), demonstrates its operational superiority over other measures in adversarial settings, connects it to algorithmic complexity (Grover's algorithm), and numerically characterizes the capacity gap scaling law.
why_interesting: For AMO physicists studying open quantum systems, this work provides a new, quantifiable metric ($F(ho)$) that detects structural information loss beyond simple entanglement or coherence measures when the system is subjected to structured noise or targeted attacks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
